title: UPS Room Sizing Guide
summary: Understand UPS footprint, battery space, service clearance, ventilation, heat, and expansion planning.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps estimate whether a room or area has enough space and support capacity for UPS equipment and batteries.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch infrastructure constraints before field work begins.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning UPS rooms, battery cabinets, power closets, backup power areas, or infrastructure support spaces.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-site validation.
Explaining physical infrastructure tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical infrastructure planning terms.

items.label: UPS Footprint
items.description: The physical floor space used by the UPS equipment.
items.whyItMatters: Footprint affects placement and service access.
items.commonMistake: Only checking electrical capacity and ignoring space.

items.label: Battery Footprint
items.description: Space used by internal or external battery systems.
items.whyItMatters: Batteries can consume more space and weight than expected.
items.commonMistake: Adding runtime without planning battery space.

items.label: Service Clearance
items.description: Working space needed for maintenance and replacement.
items.whyItMatters: UPS and batteries need service room.
items.commonMistake: Placing UPS equipment where batteries cannot be safely replaced.

items.label: Heat Output
items.description: Heat generated by UPS losses and battery systems.
items.whyItMatters: Heat affects ventilation and room temperature.
items.commonMistake: Assuming backup power equipment produces no heat.

items.label: Ventilation / Cooling
items.description: Room cooling or airflow available for UPS equipment.
items.whyItMatters: Poor cooling can reduce equipment and battery life.
items.commonMistake: Installing UPS equipment in a hot closet.

items.label: Growth Allowance
items.description: Future UPS, battery, or load expansion.
items.whyItMatters: Backup power needs often grow after installation.
items.commonMistake: Designing a UPS area with no spare space.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the UPS room appears practical. Watch means clearance, heat, or growth should be reviewed. Risk means the room may be too constrained for safe service or expansion.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add space, capacity, clearance, or validation before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most infrastructure planning problems come from checking whether equipment fits physically while ignoring service access, growth, pathway space, cooling, weight, or real installation conditions.
bullets: Sizing only for the initial install.
Ignoring service clearance and future access.
Using theoretical capacity as if it were practical capacity.
Failing to document assumptions before construction or installation begins.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, code requirements, structural review, electrical review, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer and code requirements still matter.
Site conditions may change the final design.
Clearance, service access, and future growth should be planned early.
A design with margin is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual equipment and site conditions.
Confirm owner requirements and operating procedures.
Document assumptions before implementation.
